description: The Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS), currently v4.0.1, published by the PCI Security Standards Council. Applies to any organization that stores, processes, or transmits cardholder data — not a law, but a contractual requirement imposed by the payment card brands (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover, JCB) through your acquiring bank/processor. Requirement wording below is paraphrased from PCI SSC's official Quick Reference Guide rather than quoted verbatim, since the standard itself is copyrighted.

source.label: PCI Security Standards Council — PCI DSS v4.0.1 Quick Reference Guide

items: PCI DSS — Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard, currently **v4.0.1** (a limited, no-new-requirements revision of v4.0 released June 2024).
Published by the **PCI Security Standards Council (PCI SSC)**, a body formed by the major card brands — not a government agency.

Applies to any entity that stores, processes, or transmits **cardholder data (CHD)** or could impact the security of the **cardholder data environment (CDE)** — merchants, processors, service providers, and more.

overview.title: Key Differences from Other Compliance Frameworks

items: **PCI DSS** — a contractual requirement imposed by the card brands through your acquiring bank, not a voluntary market-driven choice. If you accept card payments, some level of PCI DSS compliance is mandatory.
**SOC 2 / ISO 27001** — voluntary; you choose to pursue them for competitive/trust reasons, not because a specific contract requires it.
**PCI DSS** — industry-mandated (by the card networks), enforced through contracts and financial penalties, not a government statute.
**HIPAA / CCPA** — actual laws, enforced by government regulators with statutory penalties.

blocks.text: PCI DSS sits in an unusual middle ground: not voluntary like SOC 2/ISO, but not government law either. Non-compliance consequences come through your merchant agreement — fines from your acquirer, increased transaction fees, or in serious cases losing the ability to accept card payments at all.

items: **Build and Maintain a Secure Network and Systems** — Requirements 1–2. Network security controls and secure configurations.
**Protect Account Data** — Requirements 3–4. Protecting stored data and data in transit.
**Maintain a Vulnerability Management Program** — Requirements 5–6. Anti-malware and secure development/patching.
**Implement Strong Access Control Measures** — Requirements 7–9. Logical access, authentication, and physical access.
**Regularly Monitor and Test Networks** — Requirements 10–11. Logging and ongoing security testing.
**Maintain an Information Security Policy** — Requirement 12. The organizational/governance layer tying everything together.

blocks.text: PCI DSS has over 300 individual testing procedures at its most granular level. This site covers the 63 first-level sub-requirements (the "X.Y" level, e.g. 3.1 through 3.7) — the level most compliance teams actually organize their programs around — rather than every deepest-level testing procedure.
See the [Controls tab](/frameworks/pci-dss/controls) for the full breakdown.

items: **Contractual requirement** — your merchant agreement with your acquiring bank requires it if you accept card payments, regardless of company size.
**Financial consequences** — non-compliance can mean fines from your acquirer, higher transaction fees, or (after a breach) liability for fraud losses and forensic investigation costs.
**Card brand mandates** — Visa, Mastercard, and the other networks can restrict or revoke your ability to process cards for serious or repeated non-compliance.

blocks.text: PCI DSS's technical controls (network segmentation, encryption, access control, logging) overlap heavily with SOC 2 and ISO 27001 — a solid PCI DSS program is rarely wasted effort even outside the card-payment context specifically.
Quick self-score across all six goals. If you're answering "no" to more than half the questions in any goal, prioritize it.

rows: Is the CDE segmented from the rest of your network with default-deny NSCs?
Are vendor default passwords/settings changed on every system before go-live?
Is stored PAN kept to a minimum and rendered unreadable (encrypted/tokenized)?
Is PAN encrypted with strong, current cryptography during transmission?
Is anti-malware deployed, active, and monitored across in-scope systems?
Are security patches applied on a defined, risk-based timeline?
Is MFA enforced for all access into the CDE, not just remote access?
Is physical access to CDE facilities and media restricted and logged?
Are audit logs centralized, protected, and actually reviewed on a defined cadence?
Do you run quarterly ASV scans and annual penetration testing?
Is your CDE scope documented, validated, and reassessed regularly?
Do you track TPSPs touching the CDE with contracts defining PCI DSS responsibilities?

items: Accurately defining CDE scope is the single most common source of both gaps and audit findings — start there before anything else.

[PCI SSC's Document Library](https://www.pcisecuritystandards.org/document_library/) has the official Quick Reference Guide and Self-Assessment Questionnaires (SAQs) matched to different merchant/service-provider types.

blocks.text: Network segmentation done properly, and building out real log monitoring/review (not just collection), consistently take the longest — both require genuine infrastructure investment and, for segmentation, often a redesign of how systems are networked, not just a configuration change.

items: **Accurate CDE scope** — everything else is sized and prioritized based on this; getting it wrong wastes effort everywhere else.
**Change default configurations** — cheap, high-impact, and usually just a checklist exercise across existing systems.
**MFA for CDE access** — a configuration change in tools you likely already run.
**Basic anti-malware and patching cadence** — foundational, low-effort relative to the risk it addresses.
Once those are in place, invest in the harder infrastructure work — network segmentation, centralized logging with real review, and a mature vulnerability/penetration testing program.

blocks.text: Timeline varies heavily by merchant/service-provider level and starting maturity — a first-time Level 1 assessment can take 6–12 months; smaller merchants completing a Self-Assessment Questionnaire (SAQ) can move much faster.

items: **Determine your level** — merchant/service-provider level (set by transaction volume) determines whether you need a Report on Compliance (ROC) via a QSA or can self-assess with an SAQ.
**Define CDE scope** — identify every system, network segment, and process that touches cardholder data.
**Gap analysis** — assess current state against all 12 requirements.
**Remediation** — implement fixes for identified gaps.
**Vulnerability scanning** — quarterly external scans via a PCI-Approved Scanning Vendor (ASV).
**Assessment** — complete the SAQ or ROC, and submit the resulting Attestation of Compliance (AOC).
**Self-Assessment Questionnaire (SAQ)** — self-attestation against a subset of requirements matched to your specific processing scenario; used by smaller merchants.
**Report on Compliance (ROC)** — a full assessment conducted by a Qualified Security Assessor (QSA), required for the highest-volume merchants and most service providers.

blocks.text: The AOC is the document you actually submit to your acquirer/the card brands — a formal declaration of your compliance status, backed by either the SAQ or ROC.
QSA assessments consistently flag the same issues: incomplete or inaccurate CDE scope, missing network segmentation validation, and logging that's collected but not actually reviewed.
PCI DSS compliance is validated annually, but the underlying controls need to operate continuously in between.
Quarterly ASV scans, ongoing internal vulnerability scanning, continuous log review, and annual penetration testing — not just an annual point-in-time assessment.
SAQ or ROC (as applicable) and the resulting AOC need to be renewed annually — compliance status doesn't carry over indefinitely.
New systems, payment channels, or processors that touch cardholder data need to be brought into CDE scope before they go live, not discovered during the next assessment.

categories.title: Install and Maintain Network Security Controls
categories.group: Build and Maintain a Secure Network and Systems
categories.summary: Network security controls (firewalls and equivalent technologies) enforce policy at the boundaries between network segments.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for installing and maintaining network security controls are defined and understood.
controls.notes: Documented, current NSC policies and procedures that staff actually follow, not just a policy binder.

controls.criteria: Network security controls are configured and maintained, including current network and data-flow diagrams.
controls.notes: Keep real, current network/data-flow diagrams showing every CDE connection — auditors ask for these first.

controls.criteria: Network access to and from the cardholder data environment is restricted to what's necessary.
controls.notes: Default-deny inbound and outbound CDE traffic, allowing only explicitly justified connections.

controls.criteria: Network connections between trusted and untrusted networks are controlled.
controls.notes: NSCs specifically between the CDE and any less-trusted network, including your own corporate network.

controls.criteria: Risks from devices that can connect to both untrusted networks and the CDE are mitigated.
controls.notes: Personal/remote devices bridging the internet and the CDE (e.g. a laptop on VPN) need their own risk controls.

categories.title: Apply Secure Configurations to All System Components
categories.group: Build and Maintain a Secure Network and Systems
categories.summary: Vendor default passwords and settings are public knowledge and a common attack vector — hardening removes that easy path in.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for applying secure configurations to all system components are defined and understood.
controls.notes: A documented configuration-hardening standard, not tribal knowledge.

controls.criteria: System components are configured and managed securely, including changing all vendor defaults.
controls.notes: Change every default password/setting before a system touches the CDE, and remove unnecessary services/accounts.

controls.criteria: Wireless environments are configured and managed securely.
controls.notes: Default wireless settings (SSIDs, keys, passwords) get changed the same as any other default before go-live.

categories.title: Protect Stored Account Data
categories.summary: Don't store account data you don't need — and render unreadable (encrypt, truncate, hash) whatever you do store.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for protecting stored account data are defined and understood.
controls.notes: A documented data retention/disposal policy specific to account data, not a generic IT policy.

controls.criteria: Storage of account data is kept to a minimum, per documented retention and disposal policies.
controls.notes: If you don't need it, don't keep it — the safest stored PAN is the one that was never stored.

controls.criteria: Sensitive authentication data (SAD) is not stored after authorization.
controls.notes: Full track data, CVV, and PIN data must be purged the moment authorization completes — no exceptions for convenience.

controls.criteria: Access to displays of full PAN and the ability to copy cardholder data are restricted.
controls.notes: Mask PAN on display by default (show only first 6/last 4) and restrict who can export/copy full values.

controls.criteria: The primary account number (PAN) is secured wherever it is stored.
controls.notes: Render stored PAN unreadable via strong cryptography, truncation, tokenization, or an equivalent method.

controls.criteria: Cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data are secured.
controls.notes: Key storage needs its own protection — a strong cipher is worthless if the key sits next to the encrypted data.

controls.criteria: Key management processes covering the full key lifecycle are defined and implemented.
controls.notes: Generation, distribution, rotation, and retirement of keys all need documented, followed procedures.

categories.title: Protect Cardholder Data with Strong Cryptography During Transmission Over Open, Public Networks
categories.summary: PAN in transit over untrusted networks is a prime interception target.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for protecting cardholder data during transmission over open, public networks are defined and documented.
controls.notes: A documented policy naming which transmission channels require encryption and how it's enforced.

controls.criteria: PAN is protected with strong cryptography during transmission.
controls.notes: TLS (current, properly configured versions) for any PAN crossing a public network — no legacy SSL/early TLS.

categories.title: Protect All Systems and Networks from Malicious Software
categories.summary: Malware is a common path from a single compromised endpoint to a full CDE breach.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for protecting systems and networks from malicious software are defined and understood.
controls.notes: A documented anti-malware policy naming which systems are in scope and how coverage is verified.

controls.criteria: Malicious software is prevented, or detected and addressed.
controls.notes: Anti-malware deployed on all systems commonly affected by malware, kept current.

controls.criteria: Anti-malware mechanisms and processes are active, maintained, and monitored.
controls.notes: Don't just install anti-malware — verify it's actually running, updating, and generating logs someone reviews.

controls.criteria: Anti-phishing mechanisms protect users against phishing attacks.
controls.notes: Email/web filtering plus user training, since phishing is the most common initial-access vector into the CDE.

categories.title: Develop and Maintain Secure Systems and Software
categories.summary: Unpatched systems and insecurely-developed applications are two of the most exploited weaknesses in the CDE.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for developing and maintaining secure systems and software are defined and understood.
controls.notes: A documented secure-development and patch-management policy, not ad hoc practice.

controls.criteria: Bespoke and custom software is developed securely.
controls.notes: Secure coding standards and training for anyone writing code that touches the CDE.

controls.criteria: Security vulnerabilities are identified and addressed.
controls.notes: A patch management process with defined timelines based on severity, applied to all in-scope systems.

controls.criteria: Public-facing web applications are protected against attacks.
controls.notes: A WAF or equivalent, plus regular testing, for any web app exposed to the internet that touches the CDE.

controls.criteria: Changes to all system components are managed securely.
controls.notes: A formal change control process — testing, approval, rollback plan — before changes reach production CDE systems.

categories.title: Restrict Access to Cardholder Data by Business Need to Know
categories.summary: Access should be the minimum necessary to do the job — nothing broader by default.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for restricting access by business need to know are defined and understood.
controls.notes: A documented access control policy tying access levels to job roles, not ad hoc grants.

controls.criteria: Access to system components and data is appropriately defined and assigned.
controls.notes: Role-based access definitions reviewed and approved, with least privilege as the default.

controls.criteria: Access to system components and data is managed via an access control system.
controls.notes: A real access control system (not a spreadsheet) enforcing the defined roles technically.

categories.title: Identify Users and Authenticate Access to System Components
categories.summary: Every action needs to trace back to one accountable person — shared accounts break that.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for identifying users and authenticating access are defined and understood.
controls.notes: A documented identity/authentication policy covering every account type, including service accounts.

controls.criteria: User identification and accounts are strictly managed throughout their lifecycle.
controls.notes: Unique IDs for every user (no shared logins), with prompt deactivation on termination or role change.

controls.criteria: Strong authentication for users and administrators is established and managed.
controls.notes: Strong password/passphrase requirements, or equivalent strong authentication, enforced technically not just by policy.

controls.criteria: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is implemented to secure access into the CDE.
controls.notes: MFA for all access into the CDE, not just remote access — this became a hard requirement, not just best practice, as of March 2025.

controls.criteria: MFA systems are configured to prevent misuse.
controls.notes: MFA implementations need to actually resist bypass (e.g. no fallback to single-factor without justification).

controls.criteria: Use of application and system accounts is strictly managed.
controls.notes: Service/system accounts need the same lifecycle discipline as human accounts — inventory, rotation, and restriction.

categories.title: Restrict Physical Access to Cardholder Data
categories.summary: Physical access to CDE systems or media bypasses every logical control you've built.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for restricting physical access to cardholder data are defined and understood.
controls.notes: A documented physical security policy scoped specifically to CDE facilities and media.

controls.criteria: Physical access controls manage entry into facilities and systems containing cardholder data.
controls.notes: Badge access or equivalent, logged, for any space housing CDE systems.

controls.criteria: Physical access for personnel and visitors is authorized and managed.
controls.notes: Visitors escorted and logged; personnel access reviewed and tied to actual job need.

controls.criteria: Media containing cardholder data is securely stored, accessed, distributed, and destroyed.
controls.notes: Physical and digital media tracked through its full lifecycle, with secure destruction before disposal.

controls.criteria: Point-of-interaction (POI) devices are protected from tampering and unauthorized substitution.
controls.notes: A maintained inventory of POI devices with periodic physical inspection for skimming hardware.

categories.title: Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
categories.summary: Without logs, you can't detect an intrusion or reconstruct what happened during one.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for logging and monitoring access are defined and documented.
controls.notes: A documented logging policy naming what's logged, where, and for how long.

controls.criteria: Audit logs support detection of anomalies, suspicious activity, and forensic analysis.
controls.notes: Logs need to capture who, what, when, and outcome for every security-relevant event, not just errors.

controls.criteria: Audit logs are protected from destruction and unauthorized modification.
controls.notes: Centralize logs somewhere the systems generating them can't tamper with their own history.

controls.criteria: Audit logs are reviewed to identify anomalies or suspicious activity.
controls.notes: Someone (or some tool) actually reviews the logs on a defined cadence — collection without review doesn't count.

controls.criteria: Audit log history is retained and available for analysis.
controls.notes: Retention periods long enough to support investigation of an incident discovered well after it happened.

controls.criteria: Time-synchronization mechanisms support consistent time settings across all systems.
controls.notes: NTP (or equivalent) across the environment, so logs from different systems can actually be correlated.

controls.criteria: Failures of critical security control systems are detected, reported, and responded to promptly.
controls.notes: Alerting when logging, NSC, anti-malware, or other critical controls themselves fail or go offline.

categories.title: Test Security of Systems and Networks Regularly
categories.summary: Vulnerabilities are discovered continuously — a control tested once at launch isn't verified to still work.

controls.criteria: Processes and mechanisms for regularly testing security of systems and networks are defined and understood.
controls.notes: A documented testing policy naming scan/pentest cadence and scope.

controls.criteria: Wireless access points are identified and monitored; unauthorized ones are addressed.
controls.notes: Regular wireless scans to catch rogue access points bridging into the CDE.

controls.criteria: External and internal vulnerabilities are regularly identified, prioritized, and addressed.
controls.notes: External scans quarterly by a PCI-Approved Scanning Vendor at minimum, plus internal scanning.

controls.criteria: External and internal penetration testing is regularly performed and findings are corrected.
controls.notes: Annual (at minimum) penetration testing with a defined remediation and retest process for findings.

controls.criteria: Network intrusions and unexpected file changes are detected and responded to.
controls.notes: Intrusion detection/prevention and file integrity monitoring on critical CDE systems.

controls.criteria: Unauthorized changes on payment pages are detected and responded to.
controls.notes: Change-detection specifically for payment page scripts, given the rise in e-skimming/Magecart-style attacks.

categories.title: Support Information Security with Organizational Policies and Programs
categories.summary: Technical controls without organizational backing (policy, training, accountability) don't hold up over time.

controls.criteria: A comprehensive information security policy is known and current.
controls.notes: One current, actually-read policy governing information security overall, not a stack of outdated documents.

controls.criteria: Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are defined and implemented.
controls.notes: Clear rules for how employees can use devices/technologies that touch or could touch the CDE.

controls.criteria: Risks to the cardholder data environment are formally identified, evaluated, and managed.
controls.notes: A documented, recurring risk assessment specific to the CDE, not just a general IT risk register.

controls.criteria: PCI DSS compliance is managed as an ongoing program.
controls.notes: Assigned ownership and a recurring cadence for maintaining compliance, not a once-a-year scramble before assessment.

controls.criteria: PCI DSS scope is documented and validated.
controls.notes: A current, validated description of exactly what's in scope — scope creep is one of the most common assessment findings.

controls.criteria: Security awareness education is an ongoing activity.
controls.notes: Regular, tracked training for anyone with access to the CDE, not a one-time onboarding video.

controls.criteria: Personnel are screened to reduce risks from insider threats.
controls.notes: Background screening proportional to the access level a role provides into the CDE.

controls.criteria: Risk from third-party service provider (TPSP) relationships is managed.
controls.notes: An inventory of TPSPs touching the CDE, with contracts defining each party's PCI DSS responsibilities.

controls.criteria: Third-party service providers support their customers' PCI DSS compliance.
controls.notes: If you're a TPSP yourself, you need to acknowledge your responsibility and provide compliance evidence to customers.

controls.criteria: Suspected and confirmed security incidents affecting the CDE are responded to immediately.
controls.notes: A tested incident response plan with defined roles, ready before an incident happens, not drafted during one.
